evidence: Server binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — explicitly restricts to 127.0.0.1/::1, preventing any external access by design. This is the primary access control mechanism.
CORS origin restricted to System.getProperty('cors.allow.origin', 'http://localhost:3000') — default only permits localhost:3000, configurable for other localhost ports.
All endpoints are read-only corpus analysis operations — no mutations, no user data, no sensitive information returned beyond corpus statistics.
No authentication or authorization layer exists — appropriate for a localhost-only research tool where network isolation IS the access control.

POST /api/bcql accepts arbitrary BCQL patterns from the request body. Pattern length (1024 chars) and complexity (20 bracket tokens) limits are enforced. If loopback binding were ever removed, this endpoint would allow arbitrary corpus queries without auth.
POST /api/visual/radial accepts arbitrary JSON up to 65536 bytes. Request body size limit is enforced.
No magic permission strings, no role-based access, no user sessions — the authorization landscape is consistently absent by design.
No admin-bypass patterns, no service-role tokens, no audit logging — not needed for a single-user localhost tool.
The CORS mechanism provides a browser-level defense against cross-origin requests, but is not a security boundary for direct HTTP clients.
All endpoints in WordSketchApiServer are registered uniformly — there are no 'some routes have auth, siblings don't' inconsistencies because auth is consistently absent.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Score capped below 90 because the entire security model depends on a single loopback binding with no defense-in-depth. Removing one constructor argument would expose all endpoints with no fallback protection.

strengths: Loopback binding (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()) is a correct and consistent access control choice for a localhost research tool.
Authorization is consistently absent rather than inconsistently present — no routes have auth while siblings don't, which is the rubric's failure mode.
Input limits (request body size, BCQL pattern length and complexity) are consistently applied to all POST endpoints.
CORS policy is applied uniformly to all responses via setCorsHeader() called from every send* helper.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: Authorization is consistently absent by design — this is appropriate for a localhost read-only tool but creates a single point of failure: the loopback binding is the only protection layer with no auth fallback if deployment context changes.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: For a localhost-only read-only corpus analysis tool, consistent absence of auth is the correct design choice — adding auth would add friction without meaningful security benefit. The score is 80 rather than 90+ because the loopback binding is the only access control layer and its removal (e.g. to add network accessibility) would immediately expose all endpoints with no auth to fall back on. The dimension asks for consistency, which is present, but also asks about whether trust boundaries are applied consistently — the single-layer defense warrants a moderate rather than high score.

issues.summary: Entire access control relies on loopback binding with no auth fallback if network accessibility is ever added
evidence: Changing InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() to new InetSocketAddress(port) would expose all endpoints with no auth mechanism in place
POST /api/bcql would allow arbitrary corpus queries against the index from any client
issues.suggestion: If this tool ever needs to be accessible beyond localhost (e.g., on a local network or as a shared research service), add an API key header check as a minimum access control layer. Document the loopback-only deployment constraint explicitly in README to signal that this is not safe to expose on a broader network without adding auth.

possible_false_positives: Any tool flagging absent auth as a finding should note this is appropriate for a localhost read-only research tool — the absence is by design, not oversight.
